title: Chained comparisons: desugaring
analysis: This task adds chained comparison desugaring to the compiler. During compilation, chained comparisons like `a < b < c` are desugared into And-chains of pairwise comparisons: `And(Lt(a,b), Lt(b,c))`. The implementation is entirely within the compiler's `compile_expr_guarded` function, which already handles BinOp compilation. The approach: (1) detect when the outer BinOp is a comparison and its left child is also a comparison BinOp, (2) flatten the left-nested chain into a list of operands and operators via `flatten_comparison_chain`, (3) compile each operand exactly once, (4) build pairwise comparison nodes and left-fold them into an And-chain. Middle expressions are compiled once and cloned — the content_hash identity ensures the eval engine's cache deduplicates evaluation. No syntax changes are needed since the parser already produces left-nested BinOp trees for `a < b < c` (parsed as `(a < b) < c`). The desugaring intercepts this at compile time. All comparison operators (< <= > >= == !=) participate in chaining. Non-comparison operators on the left side do not trigger chaining. The implementation already exists on main: helper functions `is_comparison_op` and `flatten_comparison_chain` (lines 638-672), and the desugaring block in `compile_expr_guarded` (lines 887-924). All 9 tests pass.

steps.description: Write failing test `simple_chain_desugars_to_and`: parse `structure S { param a/b/c : Int; constraint a < b < c }`, assert the constraint expr is `BinOp(And, BinOp(Lt, ValueRef(a), ValueRef(b)), BinOp(Lt, ValueRef(b), ValueRef(c)))`. This verifies the basic two-operator chain desugaring.

Add `is_comparison_op(op: &str) -> bool` helper that returns true for < <= > >= == !=. Add `flatten_comparison_chain` function that recursively flattens left-nested comparison BinOps into (operands, operators) vectors. In `compile_expr_guarded`'s BinOp arm, detect when outer op is comparison and left child is also comparison BinOp — call flatten_comparison_chain, compile each operand once, build pairwise BinOp nodes, and left-fold into And-chain. Step-1 test passes.

Write failing test `mixed_operators_chain_preserves_ops`: parse `constraint a < b <= c`, assert desugared to `And(Lt(a,b), Le(b,c))`. Both operator variants are preserved in the desugared output — not collapsed to a single op type.

Verify mixed operators work. The `flatten_comparison_chain` stores each operator separately, and `resolve_binop` maps each string operator to the correct BinOp variant. Step-3 test should pass with the step-2 implementation.

Write failing test `three_operator_chain_left_folds`: parse `constraint a < b < c < d`, assert desugared to `And(And(Lt(a,b), Lt(b,c)), Lt(c,d))` — a left-folded And structure with 3 pairwise comparisons, verifying arbitrary chain length support.

Verify 3-operator chain. The recursive `flatten_comparison_chain` handles arbitrary depth, and the left-fold loop `acc = BinOp(And, acc, pair)` naturally produces left-nested And trees. Step-5 test should pass.
Write failing test `equality_chain_desugars_to_and`: parse `constraint a == b == c`, assert desugared to `And(Eq(a,b), Eq(b,c))`. All comparison operators participate in chaining, including equality.
Verify equality chaining. The `is_comparison_op` function includes `==` and `!=`, so equality operators participate in the same desugaring path. Step-7 test should pass.

Write failing test `middle_expression_compiled_once`: parse `constraint a < b + c < d`, assert desugared to `And(Lt(a, Add(b,c)), Lt(Add(b,c), d))` where both occurrences of `b + c` have identical `content_hash`. This verifies middle expressions are compiled once and cloned (not recompiled).

Verify middle expression deduplication. The implementation compiles all operands into `compiled_operands` vec first, then uses `.clone()` when building pairwise comparisons. Since `CompiledExpr::binop()` derives content_hash deterministically from op+children, cloned exprs have identical hashes. Step-9 test should pass.

Write failing test `arithmetic_on_left_does_not_chain`: parse `constraint (a + b) < c`, assert result is plain `BinOp(Lt, Add(a,b), c)` — no And-wrapping. Non-comparison binary ops on the left must not trigger chaining.

Verify non-chain guarding. The `is_comparison_op` check on the inner op ensures only comparison BinOps trigger chaining. When the left child is Add/Sub/Mul/etc, the normal BinOp compilation path runs. Step-11 test should pass.

Write failing test `single_comparison_stays_plain`: parse `constraint a < b`, assert result is plain `BinOp(Lt, ValueRef(a), ValueRef(b))` — no desugaring. Regression test ensuring desugaring only activates for actual chains (>2 operands).

Verify single comparison passthrough. The chaining guard requires both the outer op AND the inner left-child op to be comparisons. A single `a < b` has an Ident left child, not a BinOp, so the guard fails and normal compilation runs. Step-13 test should pass.

Write failing test `scalar_range_constraint`: parse `constraint 2mm < thickness < 10mm` with Scalar param, assert no errors, result type is Bool, and top-level op is And. Real-world range constraint with dimensional quantities.

Verify scalar range constraint. QuantityLiteral operands compile to Scalar literals via `unit_to_scalar`, and comparison ops produce Bool result_type via `infer_binop_type`. The chaining desugaring is type-agnostic — it works on any operand types. Step-15 test should pass.

Write failing test `let_binding_chain_desugars_to_and`: parse `let in_range = 0 < x < 100`, assert the `in_range` value cell's `default_expr` is an And-chain with result_type Bool. Verifies desugaring works in let-binding context, not just constraint context.

Verify let-binding context. The `compile_expr_guarded` function is called uniformly for all expression contexts (constraints, let defaults, guard conditions). The chaining desugaring operates at the expression level, so it applies regardless of where the expression appears. Step-17 test should pass.

design_decisions.decision: Desugar at compile time, not parse time
design_decisions.rationale: The parser already produces left-nested BinOp trees for `a < b < c` (parsed as `(a < b) < c`). Desugaring in the compiler keeps the parser simple and syntax-focused. The compiler has the context to compile operands, infer types, and build proper And-chains with correct content_hashes.

design_decisions.decision: Clone middle operands instead of synthetic let bindings
design_decisions.rationale: Middle expressions are compiled once into a CompiledExpr, then cloned for each pairwise comparison that uses them. This is simpler than introducing synthetic let bindings and achieves the same guarantee: no recompilation. The eval engine caches by content_hash, so cloned expressions with identical hashes are evaluated once at runtime too.

design_decisions.decision: Left-fold And-chain structure: And(And(a<b, b<c), c<d)
design_decisions.rationale: Consistent with how the parser naturally nests left-recursive BinOps. The left-fold means the And-chain grows from the left, matching the evaluation order (short-circuit left to right). Also matches how users read the chain: `a < b` is checked first, then `b < c`, then `c < d`.

design_decisions.decision: All six comparison operators participate in chaining
design_decisions.rationale: The is_comparison_op function includes < <= > >= == !=. This matches Python's semantics and the task spec. Mixed chains like `a < b <= c` or `a == b == c` are valid. Even `a < b > c` is syntactically valid (though semantically unusual). No-op desugaring for non-comparison chains is enforced by the is_comparison_op guard.

design_decisions.decision: Flatten recursively before building pairwise nodes
design_decisions.rationale: The `flatten_comparison_chain` function recursively collects all operands and operators from the left-nested tree before building any compiled nodes. This two-pass approach (flatten then build) ensures each operand is compiled exactly once, even in long chains like `a < b < c < d < e`.

reuse.how: Used to build each pairwise comparison node and each And-combination node. The constructor deterministically derives content_hash from op + child hashes, which is critical for the middle-expression deduplication test.
Used to convert each string operator from the flattened chain into a BinOp enum variant. Already handles all comparison operators.
Used to determine the result type of each pairwise comparison node. Comparison ops always return Type::Bool, and And also returns Type::Bool.
Standard test helper that parses source, compiles, and returns the first TopologyTemplate. Reused across all 9 tests in this file, consistent with patterns in other compiler test files.
The chained comparison guard is inserted at the top of the BinOp arm, before the normal BinOp compilation path. On non-chain cases, execution falls through to the existing code unchanged.
